title: 1 Kings 13
subtitle: The Man of God from Judah; the Old Prophet's Deception

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — The Man of God Confronts the Altar at Bethel

heb.paragraph: The anonymous prophet from Judah is calledʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm(v.1) — a title of prophetic authority. He prophesies against Jeroboam's altar by name: "Josiah" — a king who will not exist for 300 years. This is the most chronologically specific predictive prophecy in Kings.

refs.note: Josiah fulfils this prophecy exactly — desecrating the Bethel altar and burning bones on it, but sparing the man of God's tomb.
Cyrus named by Isaiah 150 years before his birth. The Josiah prophecy follows the same pattern of divinely specific foreknowledge.

notes.note: Calvin: God names Josiah three centuries early to show that his purposes are certain before they are enacted. The future is not contingent from God's perspective; it is appointed. This is comfort for the faithful and warning for the faithless.

Calvin: The withered hand teaches that human power cannot resist divine authority. Jeroboam reaches out to seize the prophet and is seized himself. The arm that would silence God's messenger is paralysed by God's power.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:yōšiyyāhû šĕmô(Josiah is his name) — the naming formula is the same used for Isaac (Gen 17:19) and Jesus (Matt 1:21). Divine naming indicates divine appointment — the person's destiny is determined before birth.
NET Note:môpēt(sign/portent) — a validating sign accompanying the prophecy. The altar splits and the ashes pour out as immediate confirmation that the long-range Josiah prophecy is truly from God.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: The specificity of naming Josiah has led some scholars to date this passage to the Josianic period (c.621 BC). However, predictive naming is attested in ANE prophetic texts, and the narrative's literary function requires the prediction to precede its fulfilment. The text claims prophetic foreknowledge, not historical retrofitting.

Wiseman: The altar splitting and ashes pouring out would have been understood as divine desecration of the sacred space. In ANE religion, the destruction of an altar signified the withdrawal of divine favour from the cult.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The Josiah prophecy creates a narrative thread that spans the entire book of Kings — from ch. 13 to 2 Kgs 23. The reader holds this unfulfilled promise across hundreds of pages, watching for its resolution. It is the narrator's longest-range narrative device.

Provan: The man of God's obedience in the first episode (refusing the king) sets up the tragedy of the second episode (accepting the old prophet's invitation). The narrator constructs the chapter as a test of prophetic fidelity — passed once, failed once, with fatal consequences.

hist.context: The unnamed man of God from Judah delivers one of the most remarkable prophecies in the OT: he names Josiah — a king born 300 years later — who will desecrate this very altar (fulfilled in 2 Kgs 23:15–18). The altar splits, Jeroboam's hand withers and is restored, but Jeroboam does not repent. The man of God refuses the king's hospitality as commanded by God: "Do not eat bread or drink water or return by the way you came."

notes.note: The narrative of Kings presents Israel's history as a theological argument: obedience to God's covenant brings blessing; disobedience brings judgment. This section continues that theme, demonstrating that God's sovereignty operates through human choices and their consequences. Every passage in Kings ultimately points to the need for a perfect King who will fulfill what no earthly monarch could.

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The practical lesson for every believer is that obedience to God's Word is the only path to genuine blessing. The kings who prospered spiritually were those who feared the Lord and walked in His ways. The same principle governs the Christian life: 'If you love Me, keep My commandments' (John 14:15). Obedience is not the means of salvation but the evidence of it.

sections.header: Verses 11–34 — The Old Prophet's Lie; the Lion on the Road

heb.paragraph: The old prophet of Bethel (v.11) is one of the most enigmatic figures in Kings. He lies to the man of God — claiming an angel told him to bring him back — and the man of God believes him. Then the old prophet genuinely prophesies the man of God's death. He is simultaneously a deceiver and a true prophet — a combination that defies simple moral categories.

The lion kills the man of God but does not eat the body or attack the donkey (vv.24–25). This supernatural restraint proves the death is divine judgment, not natural accident. The lion is God's instrument — deployed with surgical precision.

refs.note: "Even if we or an angel from heaven should preach a gospel other than the one we preached to you, let them be under God's curse!" Paul's principle matches the lesson of ch. 13 exactly: no secondary authority overrides God's direct word.
Another prophet killed by a lion for disobedience — the same pattern of lethal consequence for prophetic unfaithfulness.

notes.note: Calvin: The man of God believed a lie because it came from a prophet. This teaches that we must never accept human authority — however impressive — over God's clear word. Scripture is the final court of appeal, not any human intermediary.

Calvin: God's judgment on his own prophet is terrifying in its severity. If God does not spare his faithful servant for one act of disobedience, how much more will he judge those who persistently rebel? The severity of God is the other face of the kindness of God.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:waykakkēš lô(he lied to him) — the narrator states the deception flatly. There is no ambiguity: the old prophet fabricated the angel story. The reader is told the truth so that the man of God's failure is seen as gullibility, not reasonable trust.

NET Note: The triple tableau — lion, donkey, body — standing together on the road functions as a supernatural sign. Normal predator behaviour (eating prey, attacking nearby animals) is suspended. The scene is a visible parable of divine judgment.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Lions were present in Palestine until the medieval period. References to lion attacks appear in Assyrian royal inscriptions and in biblical texts (2 Kgs 17:25–26). The supernatural element is not the lion's presence but its selective behaviour.

Wiseman: The old prophet's request to be buried beside the man of God is a recognition of the Judean prophet's genuine authority. By sharing a tomb, the old prophet ensures that when Josiah eventually desecrates the Bethel cemetery, his bones will be protected alongside the man of God's (2 Kgs 23:17–18).

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The chapter's moral is not that prophets are untrustworthy but that no human authority — even prophetic — supersedes God's direct command. The Deuteronomistic historian is establishing a principle that will govern the entire prophetic narrative of Kings: test every word against God's revealed will.

Provan: "This was the sin that led to its downfall" — the narrator delivers the dynasty's death sentence while Jeroboam is still alive. The verdict precedes the execution by decades. The reader knows the end from the middle.

hist.context: The second half of ch. 13 is one of the strangest narratives in Scripture. The old prophet lies, the man of God disobeys, the man of God is killed by a lion, and the old prophet mourns him and asks to be buried alongside him. The moral complexity is deliberate: the man of God who resisted a king's invitation falls for a prophet's lie. The lesson is severe: obedience to God's direct word cannot be overridden by any human authority, even prophetic authority. The chapter closes with the note that Jeroboam "did not change his evil ways" — the man of God's mission succeeded prophetically but failed to produce repentance.

notes.note: Elijah's ministry demonstrates that God never leaves Himself without a witness, even in the darkest periods of apostasy. The prophet's bold confrontation of idolatry foreshadows Christ's own cleansing of the temple and His uncompromising call to exclusive worship of the true God. God's servants must be willing to stand alone when faithfulness demands it.

The prophetic ministry serves as God's corrective voice within the covenant community. When kings failed and priests compromised, God raised up prophets to call His people back to faithfulness. The prophetic word always stands in judgment over human authority, reminding us that God's truth is not subject to political convenience or popular opinion (2 Tim 4:2-4).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThat same day the man of God gave a sign: \u201cThis is the sign the Lord has declared: The altar will be split apart and the ashes on it will be poured out.\u201dESVAnd he gave a sign the same day, saying, \u201cThis is the sign that the LORD has spoken: \u2018Behold, the altar shall be torn down, and the ashes that are on it shall be poured out.\u2019\u201dNIVWhen King Jeroboam heard what the man of God cried out against the altar at Bethel, he stretched out his hand from the altar and said, \u201cSeize him!\u201d But the hand he stretched out toward the man shriveled up, so that he could not pull it back.ESVAnd when the king heard the saying of the man of God, which he cried against the altar at Bethel, Jeroboam stretched out his hand from the altar, saying, \u201cSeize him.\u201d And his hand, which he stretched out against him, dried up, so that he could not draw it back to himself.

themes.note: The Man of God from Judah; the Old Prophet's Deception

coaching.tip: The man of God obeys God's word, then disobeys it when an older prophet lies to him. A lion kills him on the road. The episode is disturbing because the punishment seems disproportionate. But the narrative's point is precise: obedience to God's direct word cannot be overridden by another prophet's claim, however credible.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on the man of God who obeyed God's word perfectly and then was deceived by an old prophet, consider asking God whether you are vulnerable to spiritual authority figures who contradict what God has clearly told you.
